perturbation.original_text: (ii) at any time from the date hereof through and including the second (2nd) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restrictive Period"), solicit, seek to hire, employ or retain (directly or indirectly, whether as an employee, consultant or otherwise) any person employed or retained by the Buyer at such time or within one (1) year prior to such solicitation, hiring, employment or retention, or otherwise materially and adversely interfere with the relationship between the Buyer and any such person, without the prior written consent of the Buyer in each instance
perturbation.changed_text: (ii) at any time from the date hereof through and including the fifth (5th) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restrictive Period"), solicit, seek to hire, employ or retain (directly or indirectly, whether as an employee, consultant or otherwise) any person employed or retained by the Buyer at such time or within one (1) year prior to such solicitation, hiring, employment or retention, or otherwise materially and adversely interfere with the relationship between the Buyer and any such person, without the prior written consent of the Buyer in each instance
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under certain state laws, particularly if it is not narrowly tailored to protect the employer's legitimate business interests. This change increases the risk of the non-compete agreement being found overly broad.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates non-compete agreements. While this is a non-solicitation clause, a five-year restriction could be viewed as a de facto non-compete, especially if it significantly impairs a person's ability to practice their profession.  A longer duration increases the likelihood a court would find it unreasonable and therefore unenforceable under California law, which strongly favors employee mobility.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to five years, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The law explanation states that a longer duration increases the likelihood a court would find it unreasonable and therefore unenforceable under California law, which strongly favors employee mobility. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' While the original text's two-year restriction might be permissible, the five-year restriction in the changed text is more likely to be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, therefore creating a legal contradiction by potentially violating BPC 16600.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Notices. Any notice, request, demand or other communication required or permitted under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been given when delivered personally, one (1) day after being sent by recognized overnight courier service, or three (3) business days after being mailed by certified mail, return receipt requested, addressed to a party at the address of such party first set forth above, or at such other address as such party may hereafter have designated by notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Notices. Any notice, request, demand or other communication required or permitted under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been given when sent by electronic mail, or facsimile to a party at the address of such party first set forth above, or at such other address as such party may hereafter have designated by notice.
perturbation.explanation: Removing options for personal delivery or certified mail, return receipt requested, and relying solely on email or fax for notice may not meet the legal requirements for proper notification in all jurisdictions, especially in situations where proof of receipt is crucial.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The E-Sign Act requires consumer consent to receive information electronically when a statute or regulation requires that information be provided in writing. By mandating solely email or fax and removing options like certified mail, the modified text potentially violates the E-Sign Act if the recipient has not affirmatively consented to electronic communication for all notices under the agreement, particularly if state law requires specific methods for delivering legally binding notices.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes options for personal delivery and certified mail, return receipt requested, relying solely on email or fax for notice. This contradicts the E-Sign Act, which requires consumer consent to receive information electronically when a statute or regulation requires that information be provided in writing. The scraped snippet mentions the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act), and while it doesn't detail specific requirements, it indicates the Act's relevance (15 U.S.C. 7001 note). By removing the 'certified mail' option, particularly 'return receipt requested', the modified text eliminates a method of delivery that provides proof of receipt, potentially violating the E-Sign Act if consent for electronic delivery isn't obtained. The act makes it difficult to have confirmation the recipient got the documentation.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Governing Law; Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of Florida and Georgia state and federal courts with respect to any action between the parties relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Governing Law; Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the courts of Dominican Republic with respect to any action between the parties relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction to the Dominican Republic for a contract between a Nevada corporation and a Colorado corporation where the assets are located in North Carolina, may be deemed unenforceable if it lacks a reasonable relationship to the transaction and is designed to circumvent U.S. law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law and Forum Selection Principles under U.S. Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that the parties' choice of law will be honored if the chosen state has a 'substantial relationship' to the parties or the transaction, or there is a 'reasonable basis' for the parties' choice. Switching the jurisdiction to the Dominican Republic when the parties and assets have minimal connection to that jurisdiction could be deemed unenforceable. US courts may disregard this clause if it is found to violate a strong public policy of a state with a more substantial relationship to the dispute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the jurisdiction to the Dominican Republic. The Law Explanation indicates that Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 requires a 'substantial relationship' or 'reasonable basis' for the choice of law. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that choosing the Dominican Republic might be unenforceable if it lacks a reasonable connection and is intended to circumvent U.S. law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between jurisdictions and states that courts determine what law to apply. Because the contract involves entities and assets located outside of the Dominican Republic, this creates a legal contradiction, as the selection of the Dominican Republic's jurisdiction could be deemed unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of Florida and Georgia state and federal courts with respect to any action between the parties relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that all disputes arising under this agreement will be resolved through mandatory arbitration, with each party responsible for their own legal costs, irrespective of the arbitration outcome.
perturbation.explanation: This modification mandates arbitration and eliminates the possibility of recovering legal costs, even for the prevailing party.  This contradicts state laws that may allow for the recovery of legal fees in certain contract disputes and may be seen as unconscionable, especially if there is a power imbalance between the parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 13-1-11(a)(11) relates to attorney fees and costs, and while it does not directly prohibit mandatory arbitration clauses in all contexts, a clause preventing the prevailing party from recovering attorney's fees, particularly in cases involving unfair or deceptive practices, could be challenged under the broader principles of the Fair Business Practices Act, as it may limit the remedies available to consumers or small businesses in disputes and potentially discourage them from pursuing legitimate claims. The lack of attorney fee recovery could also render the arbitration clause unconscionable under Georgia law, depending on the specific facts and circumstances.

Previous Next Obligations to pay attorney's fees upon any note or other evidence of indebtedness, in addition to the rate of interest specified therein, shall be valid and enforceable and collectable as a part of such debt if such note or other evidence of indebtedness is collected by or through an attorney after maturity, subject to subsection (b) of this Code section and to the following provisions: If such note or other evidence of indebtedness provides for attorney's fees in some specific percent of the principal and interest owing thereon, such provision and obligation shall be valid and enforceable up to but not in excess of 15 percent of the principal and interest owing on said note or other evidence of indebtedness; If such note or other evidence of indebtedness provides for the payment of reasonable attorney's fees without specifying any specific percent, such provision shall be construed to mean 15 percent of the first $500.00 of principal and interest owing on such note or other evidence of indebtedness and 10 percent of the amount of principal and interest owing thereon in excess of $500.00; and The holder of the note or other evidence of indebtedness or his or her attorney at law shall, after maturity of the obliga
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates arbitration and removes the possibility of recovering legal costs, irrespective of the arbitration outcome. This creates a potential contradiction with Georgia law, specifically O.C.G.A. § 13-1-11, which addresses attorney's fees in debt-related obligations. While the law snippet primarily focuses on notes and evidence of indebtedness, the Law Explanation suggests that preventing the recovery of attorney's fees could render the arbitration clause unconscionable under Georgia law, especially in cases involving unfair practices. The original contract allowed for court resolution where attorney fees could potentially be awarded to the prevailing party. The modified contract's denial of legal costs to even a prevailing party may discourage legitimate claims, violating the implied principles of fairness and potentially rendering the arbitration clause unenforceable.
